basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: al-Wāqiʿa — “The Event” — paints the irruption of the Hour and the great sorting of humanity into three troops: the foremost brought-near, the people of the right, the people of the left. Qushayrī opens with a basmala on the Name of the All-Powerful who “draws to Himself by His kindness whoever entrusts himself to Him, and constrains by the force of His authority whoever persists.” He dwells on the brought-near (muqarrabūn) — not “those who draw near” but “the brought-near,” for nearness (qurb) is a gift, not a conquest. The sūra unfolds in mirror the felicity of the elect (gardens, springs, houris, peace) and the torment of the deniers (Zaqqūm, boiling water, scorching wind), then summons four signs of creation — the seed, the crop, the water, the fire — all leading to the same Author. It culminates on the death-agony scene, where Qushayrī sets a major pearl: “We are nearer to him than you, but you do not see” — the greatest nearness comes when the servant fades and sees only the Real. It is sealed on the “truth of certainty” (ḥaqq al-yaqīn) and the glorification of the Name of the Magnificent.
themes: the divine nearness at the death-agony

versets.trad: When the Event befalls,
versets.commentaire: The sūra opens on the irruption of the Hour, named al-Wāqiʿa — “that which falls,” what comes about without recourse. Qushayrī: when the Resurrection rises, nothing can repel it. And he draws the spiritual sense: when the Event befalls, whoever followed the path of uprightness reaches safety and meets honour; whoever swerved from rectitude falls into regret and loss — “when tears intermingle on cheeks, the one who weeps is told apart from the one who feigns weeping.”

mots.racine: و ق ع
mots.def: wāqiʿa: that which inevitably comes about, the Resurrection.

versets.trad: none can deny its coming.
versets.commentaire: No denial holds before its coming. Qushayrī: kādhiba is here a verbal noun, like ʿāfiya — it is true, nothing repels it, and there is in its coming no lie. What men denied then reveals itself with a truth nothing can gainsay.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / و ق ع

versets.trad: abasing, exalting.
versets.commentaire: The Hour is “abasing, exalting” (khāfiḍa, rāfiʿa). Qushayrī unfolds its pairs: abasing for the people of wretchedness, exalting for the people of accord; abasing for the souls, exalting for the hearts; abasing for the people of desire, exalting for the people of election; abasing for whoever denies, exalting for whoever proclaims the One.

mots.racine: خ ف ض / ر ف ع
mots.def: khāfiḍa: that lowers; rāfiʿa: that raises.

versets.trad: When the earth is shaken violently,
versets.commentaire: The earth is shaken with a violent shaking (rajj). The very ground, image of stability, loses its footing. The great upheaval begins where man thought to set his feet in safety.

mots.racine: ر ج ج

versets.trad: and the mountains are crumbled to pieces
versets.commentaire: The mountains are crumbled (bass). Qushayrī: ground down until they become like the dust that floats in a sunbeam coming through a skylight. What was most massive and most rooted becomes impalpable dust.

mots.racine: ب س س / ج ب ل
mots.def: bassa: to crumble, reduce to powder.

versets.trad: and become scattered dust,
versets.commentaire: They become scattered dust (habāʾ munbathth) — the fine motes seen only in a ray of light. The apparent grandeur of the world resolves into nothing: what seemed eternal was but dust in suspension.

mots.racine: ه ب و / ب ث ث

versets.trad: you will be three kinds.
versets.commentaire: Humanity is divided into three classes (azwāj): those of the right, those of the left, and the foremost. The cosmic upheaval issues in a spiritual sorting: the end of the world reveals what each truly was.

mots.racine: ز و ج

versets.trad: The companions of the right — who are the companions of the right?
versets.commentaire: The people of the right (al-maymana) — the people of good fortune and blessing. Qushayrī: the phrase “who are they?” is an exaltation of their rank. These, he says, are those who stood at Adam's right on the Day of the primordial Covenant (yawm al-dharr).

mots.racine: ي م ن
ي م ن
mots.sens: who are the people of the right?

versets.trad: And the companions of the left — who are the companions of the left?
versets.commentaire: The people of the left (al-mashʾama) — the people of ill omen, baleful to themselves. Qushayrī: the phrase here is an abasement, as much as it was an exaltation for the people of the right. These are those who stood at Adam's left at the primordial Covenant.

mots.racine: ش أ م
ش أ م
mots.sens: who are the people of the left?

versets.trad: And the foremost, the foremost,
versets.commentaire: The foremost (al-sābiqūn) form the third troop — the highest. Qushayrī piles up readings: the foremost toward the fair virtues; toward emigration; toward Islam; toward the five prayers; by the truthfulness of their step; by loftiness of aspiration; toward every good; those who hasten to repent of faults. And: those for whom the beautiful preceded from God, and who raced toward what had raced toward them.

mots.racine: س ب ق
mots.def: sābiqūn: those who outstrip, who precede.

versets.trad: those are the ones brought near,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī dwells on the divine precision: He did not say “those who draw near” (al-mutaqarribūn) but “those brought near” (al-muqarrabūn) — it is the pure effect of the divine drawing-near. All creation then knows that they outstripped by the nearness of their Lord, not by their own walking. Nearness (qurb) is a gift, not a conquest.

mots.racine: ق ر ب

versets.trad: in the Gardens of delight.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī subtilizes: “brought near” — but to what? It is absurd that they be in Paradise then brought near to Paradise; they are brought near to other than Paradise: to the carpet of nearness. And he specifies: brought near not by distance but by honour; their souls drawn near to Paradise, their hearts drawn near to the Real — from the carpet of gnosis, their spirits to the courts of contemplation.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ع م
mots.def: jannāt al-naʿīm: the gardens of felicity.

versets.trad: a multitude of the former peoples
versets.commentaire: A multitude (thulla) of the former. Qushayrī: a throng of the former who saw their prophets, and few of the later who saw our Prophet; or: a throng of the predecessors (salaf) and few of the successors of the community. The foremost are mostly of the early times, when the light was new.

mots.racine: ث ل ل / أ و ل
mots.def: thulla: a numerous group; awwalūn: the former.

versets.trad: and few of the later peoples,
versets.commentaire: And few (qalīl) of the later. The rank of the foremost grows rare as time recedes from the prophetic source. But this “few” keeps all its dignity: rarity does not lessen height.

mots.racine: ق ل ل / أ خ ر

versets.trad: on couches wrought [of gold],
versets.commentaire: On wrought couches (mawḍūna), woven of gold like a coat of mail. Qushayrī reports an image: the couch, if he wishes to sit, lowers; if he stretches out, rises. The repose of the brought-near answers their desire before they even voice it.

mots.racine: س ر ر / و ض ن

versets.trad: reclining on them, facing one another.
versets.commentaire: Reclining and facing one another (mutaqābilīn). Qushayrī: none sees another's back — an image of pure affection and noble character. In Paradise, gazes meet without evasion: the transparency of hearts abolishes all distance.

mots.racine: و ك أ / ق ب ل

versets.trad: Immortal youths circle around them
versets.commentaire: Immortal young servants (mukhalladūn) circle around them. Qushayrī: they circle while the blessed remain, not stirring; youths of one age, who do not grow old. The service of Paradise is without wear or end: youth there is a permanent state.

mots.racine: ط و ف / و ل د / خ ل د

versets.trad: with goblets, ewers, and a cup of flowing wine
versets.commentaire: Handleless cups (akwāb), ewers (abārīq), and a cup from a flowing spring (maʿīn). The wine of Paradise gushes from a running spring, not pressed nor murky: pure from the source, like the grace that needs no human toil to give itself.

mots.racine: ك و ب / ب ر ق
ك أ س / م ع ن

versets.trad: that gives them neither headache nor intoxication,
versets.commentaire: This wine gives neither headache (ṣudāʿ) nor loss of mind. The delight of Paradise is a joy without reverse: it does not cloud the mind, leaves no heaviness. Every earthly pleasure carries its shadow; that of the Hereafter is pure light.

mots.racine: ص د ع
ن ز ف
mots.def: nazifa: to lose the mind through drink.

versets.trad: offering fruit of their choosing
versets.commentaire: Fruit of their choosing (yatakhayyarūn). The delight is not imposed but offered at the pleasure of desire: the freedom of taste is part of the felicity. Nothing there constrains; all responds to the elect's aspiration.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / خ ي ر

versets.trad: and the flesh of birds as they desire.
versets.commentaire: The flesh of birds as they desire (yashtahūn). Desire itself, which on earth is often a source of trouble, becomes in Paradise pure fulfilled gladness. What was lack becomes plenitude.

mots.racine: ل ح م / ط ي ر / ش ه و

versets.trad: And houris with wide eyes,
versets.commentaire: Houris with wide eyes (ʿīn). The word ḥūr denotes the intense white and black of the eye; ʿīn, the breadth of the gaze. The beauty of Paradise culminates in the gaze — where the soul makes itself visible.

mots.racine: ح و ر / ع ي ن
mots.def: ḥūr: of intense eye-contrast; ʿīn: wide-eyed.

versets.trad: like the hidden pearl,
versets.commentaire: Like the preserved pearl (luʾluʾ maknūn). Qushayrī: maknūn is what is kept sheltered. The hidden pearl is intact because none has touched it: preserved beauty draws its worth from its inviolate purity.

mots.racine: ل أ ل أ / ك ن ن

versets.trad: in reward for what they used to do.
versets.commentaire: All this delight is “in reward for what they did” (jazāʾ). But Qushayrī recalled that the brought-near are so by the divine drawing-near, not by their own walking: the reward of deeds is itself but a grace, for it is God who first gave the power to act.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ع م ل
mots.sens: in reward for what they did

versets.ar: لَا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا تَأْثِيمًا
versets.tr: lā yasmaʿūna fīhā laghwan wa-lā taʾthīman
versets.trad: They will hear there no idle talk nor sinful speech,
versets.commentaire: They hear there neither idle talk (laghw) nor sinful word (taʾthīm). Qushayrī: laghw is the false in speech, taʾthīm sin and raving. Paradise is first a place of peace of language: no word there wounds, lies, or defiles.

mots.racine: س م ع / ل غ و
mots.sens: they hear there no idle talk

mots.racine: أ ث م
mots.def: taʾthīm: that which causes sin, insult.

versets.trad: but only the word: “Peace! Peace!”
versets.commentaire: Only one word: “Peace! Peace!” (salām). All the language of Paradise is summed up in this word, which is also one of the divine Names (al-Salām). Where all vain speech ceases, the only salutation that brings calm remains: the peace that comes from God and leads back to Him.

mots.racine: ق و ل / س ل م

versets.trad: And the companions of the right — who are the companions of the right?
versets.commentaire: An exalting reprise of the people of the right (aṣḥāb al-yamīn). The sūra now turns to their description, after that of the brought-near: to each rank its felicity, and the divine generosity forgets none of the classes of the blessed.

mots.racine: ي م ن

versets.trad: They will be among thornless lote-trees
versets.commentaire: Among thornless lote-trees (sidr makhḍūd). The thorny tree of this world becomes, in Paradise, a tree stripped of all wounding. The favour there is purified of all that could harm: sweetness without the prick.

mots.racine: س د ر / خ ض د

versets.trad: and clustered banana-trees,
versets.commentaire: And acacias (or banana-trees) with clustered fruit (manḍūd). Qushayrī: the ṭalḥ is the banana-tree, whose fruits are stacked one upon another. Abundance there is arranged in order: the profusion of Paradise is no chaos but harmony.

mots.racine: ط ل ح / ن ض د

versets.trad: under extended shade,
versets.commentaire: An extended shade (mamdūd). Qushayrī: like the time between dawn and sunrise — the perpetual coolness of a moment that does not pass; also: a shade that endures. In Paradise, the sweetness of morning shade is without end.

mots.racine: ظ ل ل / م د د

versets.trad: beside flowing water,
versets.commentaire: Outpoured water (maskūb). Qushayrī: flowing, without their having to toil for it. Water, on earth a source of labour, here flows of itself: the gift precedes the need and demands no effort.

mots.racine: م و ه / س ك ب

versets.trad: abundant fruit
versets.commentaire: Abundant fruit (kathīra). The profusion is itself a sign of the divine munificence. Where this world measures and counts, Paradise overflows without reckoning.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / ك ث ر

versets.trad: neither cut off nor forbidden,
versets.commentaire: Neither cut off (maqṭūʿa) nor forbidden (mamnūʿa). Qushayrī: not severed from them, nor forbidden to them. The favour of Paradise knows neither dead season nor prohibition: it is offered without break or barrier, unlike the gifts of this world, always limited.

mots.racine: ق ط ع / م ن ع

versets.trad: resting on elevated couches.
versets.commentaire: Elevated couches (marfūʿa). Qushayrī notes that the spouses are also intended, by metonymy. The elevation of the couches speaks the height of the granted repose: a repose that lifts the being above all toil.

mots.racine: ف ر ش / ر ف ع

versets.trad: Truly, We brought them into a new creation,
versets.commentaire: God created them in a new creation (inshāʾ). Qushayrī: these are the wide-eyed houris. The creation of Paradise is not the prolongation of that of this world: it is a new instauration, where the being is remade in its perfection.

mots.racine: ن ش أ
mots.sens: We brought them into a new creation
mots.def: anshaʾa: to bring into being, instaurate.

versets.trad: and made them virgins,
versets.commentaire: Made virgins (abkār). Inviolate purity is their permanent state: the new creation makes them ever fresh. Virginity figures here the freshness of a gift that nothing has marred.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ب ك ر

versets.trad: loving, of equal age
versets.commentaire: Loving (ʿurub) and of one age (atrāb). Qushayrī: ʿurub are the tender spouses, enamoured of their husband; atrāb, those of equal youth. Love and harmony of age speak a perfect concord, without the least dissonance.

mots.racine: ع ر ب
mots.def: ʿurub: tender and enamoured of their husband.

mots.racine: ت ر ب

versets.trad: for the companions of the right,
themes: for the people of the right
versets.commentaire: All this is destined for the people of the right. The recapitulation insists: the felicity described is no vague promise but a named gift, assigned to a precise people — those who will have merited the right.

mots.racine: ي م ن
mots.sens: for the people of the right

versets.trad: a multitude of the former peoples
versets.commentaire: A multitude of the former. Qushayrī: a throng of the former of this community. Unlike the foremost (verses 13-14), the people of the right are numerous on both sides of time: the way of the right is broad and welcomes the multitude.

mots.racine: ث ل ل / أ و ل

versets.trad: and a multitude of the later peoples.
versets.commentaire: And a multitude of the later. Qushayrī: a throng of the later of this community. The door of the right stays widely open until the last of times: none is excluded by his generation from this felicity.

mots.racine: ث ل ل / أ خ ر

versets.trad: And the companions of the left — who are the companions of the left?
versets.commentaire: Now the people of the left (aṣḥāb al-shimāl). The phrase “who are they?”, exalting for the right, becomes here an announcement of doom. The contrast is about to break: to the felicity described answers, in mirror, the torment.

mots.racine: ش م ل

versets.trad: In a scorching wind and boiling water,
themes: the scorching wind and boiling water
versets.commentaire: In a torrid wind (samūm) and boiling water (ḥamīm). Qushayrī: the samūm is the burning breath of Hell; the ḥamīm, hot water. Instead of the extended shade of the people of the right, the breath of fire; instead of the gushing water, the water that scalds.

mots.racine: س م م / ح م م
mots.sens: in a scorching wind and boiling water
mots.def: samūm: torrid wind; ḥamīm: boiling water.

versets.trad: in the shade of black smoke,
versets.commentaire: A shade of black smoke (yaḥmūm). To the extended, cool shade of the right answers the suffocating shade of a tenebrous smoke. The shade, which should shelter, becomes here an instrument of torment.

mots.racine: ظ ل ل / ح م م

versets.trad: neither cool nor beneficial.
versets.commentaire: This shade is “neither cool nor beneficial” (lā bārid wa-lā karīm). Qushayrī: no repose in it, no good for them, for no profit is found in it. The absolute counter-gift: a shade that gives no shade, a shelter that does not shelter.

mots.racine: ب ر د / ك ر م

versets.trad: Before this they lived in luxury
versets.commentaire: They lived in opulence (mutrafīn). Qushayrī: they were pampered in this world. Luxury without gratitude is the root of their fall: the enjoyment for which one does not give thanks becomes a trap that closes the heart.

mots.racine: ت ر ف
mots.sens: before this they lived in luxury
mots.def: mutraf: who lives in ease, luxury.

versets.trad: and persisted in the greatest sin,
versets.commentaire: They persisted (yuṣirrūn) in the great sin (ḥinth ʿaẓīm). Qushayrī: the ḥinth ʿaẓīm is the enormous sin. It is not the fault alone that ruins, but obstinacy (iṣrār): the refusal to return, the hardened heart that locks itself into evil.

mots.racine: ص ر ر / ح ن ث / ع ظ م
mots.sens: they persisted in the great sin

versets.ar: وَكَانُوا يَقُولُونَ أَئِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَامًا أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ
versets.tr: wa-kānū yaqūlūna a-idhā mitnā wa-kunnā turāban wa-ʿiẓāman a-innā la-mabʿūthūna
versets.trad: saying: “When we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we really be resurrected,
versets.commentaire: Their major sin was the denial of resurrection: “once dust and bones, shall we be raised?” Qushayrī: they denied the resurrection. It is this denial that undermines all: to deny the Hereafter is to think oneself free of all reckoning.

mots.racine: م و ت / ت ر ب / ع ظ م
ب ع ث

versets.trad: we and our forefathers of old...?”
versets.commentaire: “And our first forefathers?” Incredulity marvels that generations dissolved for ages could live again. But the following verse will answer: for Him who creates, to resurrect is but the same act renewed.

mots.racine: أ ب و / أ و ل

versets.trad: Say: “Truly, those of old and those of later times
versets.commentaire: Reply: all, first and last, will be gathered. The resurrection spares nor forgets no generation. He who doubted that his forefathers would live again learns that all humanity, from first to last, will appear together.

mots.racine: أ و ل / أ خ ر

versets.trad: will be gathered at the appointed time of a known day.”
versets.commentaire: Gathered at the rendezvous (mīqāt) of a day known to God alone. The Hour has its exact term, fixed from eternity. The denial of the unbelievers changes nothing of this rendezvous: it is inscribed in the decree, and none will miss it.

mots.racine: ج م ع / و ق ت / ع ل م
mots.sens: gathered at the appointed time of a known day

versets.trad: Then you, O straying deniers,
versets.commentaire: The apostrophe to the “straying deniers” (al-ḍāllūn al-mukadhdhibūn). Straying (ḍalāl) and denial (takdhīb) go together: one denies the truth only after turning from the path. These two words sum up the root of their loss.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / ك ذ ب

versets.trad: will eat from the tree of Zaqqūm;
versets.commentaire: They will eat from the Zaqqūm tree. Qushayrī reports: the Zaqqūm is a tree at the bottom of Hell; when the denier is cast into Hell, he reaches it only after forty autumns. The infernal tree is the exact opposite of the lote-trees and banana-trees of the right: food of torment instead of delight.

mots.racine: أ ك ل / ش ج ر / ز ق م
mots.sens: you will eat of the Zaqqūm tree
mots.def: zaqqūm: infernal tree of bitter fruit.

versets.trad: you will fill your bellies with it;
versets.commentaire: They will fill their bellies with it. The hunger of torment is appeased only by what aggravates the torment: the belly fills with bitterness. A striking image of an appetite that feeds on its own punishment.

mots.racine: م ل أ / ب ط ن

versets.trad: then you will drink boiling water over it;
versets.commentaire: On top, they will drink boiling water (ḥamīm). Qushayrī: a drink from which you will draw no satisfaction. To the bitter food is added the scalding drink: the torment redoubles instead of calming.

mots.racine: ش ر ب / ح م م

versets.trad: you will drink as the thirsty camels drink.
versets.commentaire: They will drink like camels stricken with an unquenchable thirst (hīm). Qushayrī: the hīm are the thirst-sick camels; also: the sand that swallows all water poured on it. The thirst of the punishment never lets itself be slaked: the more one drinks, the more one burns.

mots.racine: ش ر ب / ه ي م

versets.trad: Such will be their hospitality on the Day of Judgment.
versets.commentaire: “Such is their welcome (nuzul) on the Day of Recompense.” The word nuzul ordinarily denotes the meal of hospitality offered to a guest: tragic irony — the hospitality reserved for the deniers is bitterness and fire. What is served them is the reverse of the banquet of the blessed.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / د ي ن
mots.sens: such is their welcome on the Day of Judgment
mots.def: nuzul: welcome-meal; yawm al-dīn: Day of Recompense.

versets.trad: It is We who created you. Why then do you not accept the truth?
versets.commentaire: “It is We who created you; why do you not affirm the truth?” Qushayrī: We created you, O people of Mecca — why did you not believe to save yourselves? You will be reproached; today you make excuses, but it will not avail you. He who could create once can re-create: to deny the resurrection is to deny the very logic of creation.

mots.racine: خ ل ق
mots.sens: it is We who created you

mots.racine: ص د ق

versets.trad: Have you considered what you emit?
versets.commentaire: “Have you considered what you emit?” The first argument drawn from creation: the drop of semen. Qushayrī develops: man's origin is from two drops; they mingle in the womb and the child forms. Meditation on one's own origin should suffice to convince of the creating power.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / م ن ي
mots.sens: have you seen what you emit

versets.trad: Is it you who create it, or are We the Creators?
versets.commentaire: “Is it you who create it, or We the Creator?” Qushayrī makes of this verse a proof of the Creator's existence: neither do the parents shape the child — their knowledge and power are too short, they who desire a child that does not come, and have one they did not want — nor does the drop shape itself, being inert. There remains only the Creator, ancient, King, Knowing.

mots.racine: خ ل ق
mots.sens: is it you who create it?

mots.racine: خ ل ق

versets.ar: نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنَا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ
versets.tr: naḥnu qaddarnā baynakumu l-mawta wa-mā naḥnu bi-masbūqīna
versets.trad: We have decreed death among you, and none can outstrip Us,
versets.commentaire: “It is We who have decreed death among you, and none outstrips Us.” Qushayrī: death befalls at the instant We will: one dies a child, one young, one aged, by diverse causes, in diverse times; nothing escapes Our decree, and We are not powerless. Death obeys a measure (taqdīr) that none thwarts.

mots.racine: ق د ر / م و ت
mots.def: qaddara: to decree, measure; mawt: death.

mots.racine: س ب ق

versets.ar: عَلَىٰ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ أَمْثَالَكُمْ وَنُنشِئَكُمْ فِي مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: ʿalā an nubaddila amthālakum wa-nunshiʾakum fī mā lā taʿlamūna
versets.trad: to replace you with your likes and bring you into being in a form you do not know.
versets.commentaire: God can replace men with their likes and re-create them in an unknown form. Qushayrī: We shall instaurate you in what you do not know — under the decree of felicity or of wretchedness. Death is not an end but a passage toward a new creation that escapes all human knowledge.

mots.racine: ب د ل / م ث ل
mots.sens: to replace you with your likes

mots.racine: ن ش أ / ع ل م
mots.sens: and bring you into being in what you know not

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولَىٰ فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ʿalimtumu n-nashʾata l-ūlā fa-lawlā tadhakkarūna
versets.trad: You have known the first creation; why then do you not reflect?
versets.commentaire: “You have known the first creation; why do you not remember?” Qushayrī: you acknowledged the first creation — why do you not recall it to know that the second is its like? The proof is before the eyes: whoever admits his own coming into being cannot deny the power to bring him back.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ن ش أ
mots.sens: you have known the first creation

mots.racine: ذ ك ر

versets.trad: Have you considered what you cultivate?
versets.commentaire: Second sign: cultivation. “Have you considered what you sow?” The familiar gesture of the ploughman becomes meditation: he casts the seed, but it is not he who makes it grow.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ح ر ث
mots.sens: have you seen what you cultivate

versets.trad: Is it you who make it grow, or are We the Growers?
versets.commentaire: “Is it you who make it grow, or We the Growers?” Qushayrī meditates the wisdom of germination: how the single seed divides into the tree born from it — its bark, its trunk, its branches, its leaves, its fruits. Man sows; God alone makes grow. The daily miracle of the grain should suffice to reveal the Creator.

mots.racine: ز ر ع
mots.sens: is it you who make it grow?

mots.racine: ز ر ع

versets.ar: لَوْ نَشَاءُ لَجَعَلْنَاهُ حُطَامًا فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ
versets.tr: law nashāʾu la-jaʿalnāhu ḥuṭāman fa-ẓaltum tafakkahūna
versets.trad: If We willed, We would make it dry chaff, and you would keep lamenting:
versets.commentaire: “If We willed, We would make it dry debris, and you would keep on marvelling.” Qushayrī: withered after its greenness, you would marvel and regret your toil and your spending. The gift of the harvest hangs on the divine will alone: let Him withhold it, and all human effort proves vain.

mots.racine: ش ي أ / ح ط م
mots.sens: if We willed, We would make it debris

mots.racine: ف ك ه

versets.trad: “We are indeed burdened with debt;
versets.commentaire: “We are indeed laden with loss!” Qushayrī: we are held to pay the loss of what we spent on the cultivation, become a charge upon us — he whose spending is gone with no return. The lost harvest figures all toil engaged without the divine blessing.

mots.racine: غ ر م

versets.trad: nay, we are deprived of everything!”
versets.commentaire: “Nay, we are deprived of everything!” Qushayrī: deprived after the sustenance has escaped us. The lament culminates in the sense of utter deprivation: without God's gift, man is but destitution, whatever his efforts.

mots.racine: ح ر م

versets.trad: Have you considered the water you drink?
versets.commentaire: Third sign: water. “Have you considered the water you drink?” The simplest and most vital element becomes object of meditation: without it, no life; and no man has made it.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / م و ه / ش ر ب
mots.sens: have you seen the water you drink

versets.ar: أَأَنتُمْ أَنزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ الْمُزْنِ أَمْ نَحْنُ الْمُنزِلُونَ
versets.tr: a-antum anzaltumūhu mina l-muzni am naḥnu l-munzilūna
versets.trad: Is it you who send it down from the clouds, or are We the Senders?
versets.commentaire: “Is it you who sent it down from the cloud, or We?” Qushayrī: We send it down when We will, where We will, as We will, on whom We will; it is We who diversify it in time, in quantity, in nature. Rain is the very image of gratuitous grace, which none provokes or masters.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / م ز ن
mots.sens: is it you who sent it down from the cloud

mots.racine: ن ز ل
mots.sens: or We who send it down?

versets.ar: لَوْ نَشَاءُ جَعَلْنَاهُ أُجَاجًا فَلَوْلَا تَشْكُرُونَ
versets.tr: law nashāʾu jaʿalnāhu ujājan fa-lawlā tashkurūna
versets.trad: If We willed, We would make it bitter. Why then are you not grateful?
versets.commentaire: “If We willed, We would make it brackish; why are you not grateful?” Sweet water is not a due but a gift: God could make it bitter. Gratitude (shukr) is the right response to grace; the forgetting to thank is already a denial.

mots.racine: ش ي أ / أ ج ج
mots.sens: if We willed, We would make it brackish

mots.racine: ش ك ر

versets.trad: Have you considered the fire you kindle?
versets.commentaire: Fourth sign: fire. “Have you considered the fire you kindle?” Qushayrī: when you strike the flint, see how the fire springs forth — is it you who create it? Even the spark wrested from dry wood is not man's work: he provokes, God produces.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ن و ر / و ر ي
mots.sens: have you seen the fire you kindle
mots.def: awrā: to strike fire (from a flint).

versets.ar: أَأَنتُمْ أَنشَأْتُمْ شَجَرَتَهَا أَمْ نَحْنُ الْمُنشِئُونَ
versets.tr: a-antum anshaʾtum shajaratahā am naḥnu l-munshiʾūna
versets.trad: Is it you who produced its tree, or are We the Producers?
versets.commentaire: “Is it you who created its tree, or We?” The tree from which fire is drawn — the markh and ʿafār of the Arabs — is not man's work. From grain to water, from water to fire, the chain of signs always leads to the same Author, whom all creation designates.

mots.racine: ن ش أ / ش ج ر
mots.sens: is it you who created its tree
mots.def: anshaʾa: to bring forth; shajara: tree.

mots.racine: ن ش أ

versets.trad: We made it a reminder and a benefit for the travellers of the desert.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We made it a reminder (tadhkira): by it man recalls the fire of Hell with which he was warned; and a benefit (matāʿ): the traveller uses it on his journey, in a thousand ways. The fire of this world is at once useful to the body and a warning to the soul: every gift carries within it a sign.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ذ ك ر
م ت ع / ق و ي
mots.def: matāʿ: enjoyment, use; muqwī: desert traveller.

versets.trad: So glorify the Name of your Lord, the Magnificent!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of this verse a call to meditation: swim by your thought in the seas of your intellect, dive by the force of tawḥīd: you will find there the jewels of knowledge. Beware of botching the dive, and beware lest doubts overrun you and engulf your capital — which is your faith. He cites: “an hour of reflection is better than a year of worship.” The glorification of the Name passes through the thought that contemplates the signs.

mots.racine: س ب ح / ع ظ م
mots.sens: glorify the Name of your Lord the Magnificent

versets.trad: I swear by the setting of the stars,
versets.commentaire: An oath by “the positions of the stars” (mawāqiʿ al-nujūm). Qushayrī: some say: the settings of the heavenly bodies; some say: the descents of the stars of the Qurʾān upon the heart of the Messenger. A double reading: the visible stars and the verses descending in fragments — the revelation is itself a constellation.

mots.racine: ق س م / و ق ع / ن ج م
mots.sens: I swear by the positions of the stars

versets.trad: and it is — if you only knew — a mighty oath:
versets.commentaire: “It is, if you knew, a mighty oath.” The grandeur of that by which God swears speaks the gravity of what He is about to affirm. Did man measure the weight of the oath, he would tremble before the truth it introduces: the nobility of the Qurʾān.

mots.racine: ق س م / ع ظ م

versets.trad: This is indeed a noble Qurʾān,
versets.commentaire: “This is a noble Qurʾān (qurʾān karīm).” Qushayrī: nobility wards off all baseness; some say: it is noble because it guides to noble virtues; some say: noble because it comes from a noble Lord, through a noble Messenger, on the tongue of a noble angel. The nobility of the Qurʾān radiates from the whole sacred chain that bears it.

mots.racine: ق ر أ / ك ر م

versets.trad: preserved in a hidden Book.
versets.commentaire: “In a preserved Book (maknūn).” Qushayrī: in the Guarded Tablet (al-lawḥ al-maḥfūẓ); some say: in the copies (maṣāḥif), and it is preserved from all alteration. The Qurʾān is kept in its heavenly origin like the pearl in its shell: intact, sheltered from all corruption.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ك ن ن

versets.trad: None may touch it but the purified.
versets.commentaire: “None touches it but the purified (al-muṭahharūn).” Qushayrī unfolds the degrees of purity: none tastes its savour and blessing but he who believes in it; none approaches it but the unitarians; none grasps its subtleties but he who has purified his secret of all other than Him; none touches its good but he who was purified, on the day of allotment, of wretchedness. The purity of the Book demands the purity of the heart that approaches it.

mots.racine: م س س / ط ه ر
mots.sens: none touch it but the purified

versets.trad: A revelation from the Lord of the worlds!
versets.commentaire: “A descent (tanzīl) from the Lord of the worlds.” The Qurʾān is no work of man: it descends (nuzūl) from the absolute source. Its nobility, its preservation, the purity it calls for — all comes from its proceeding directly from the Lord of the universes.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.sens: revelation from the Lord of the worlds

versets.trad: Is it this Discourse that you hold in disdain,
versets.commentaire: “Is it before this Discourse that you dissemble?” Qushayrī: is it of this Qurʾān that you make yourselves hypocrites (mudhin), the very one you deny? The verb dahana denotes lukewarm flattery, the complaisance that does not engage the heart. To receive the Qurʾān with the lips alone is already to betray it.

mots.racine: ح د ث / د ه ن
mots.sens: is it this Discourse you treat with lukewarmness
mots.def: ḥadīth: discourse; mudhin: who flatters, dissembles.

versets.trad: and make your provision the denial of it?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives two senses: when they were rained upon, they said: “it is by such a star” — would you, in place of gratitude for God's favour, make its denial, believing the rain comes from the stars? Or: would you make your share of the Qurʾān the denial? To make denial one's nourishment is to confuse the gift with what denies it.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ر ز ق
ك ذ ب

versets.trad: Why then, when the soul reaches the throat of the dying,
versets.commentaire: “Why do you not, when the soul reaches the throat...?” The verse opens the scene of the death-agony. Qushayrī: at the instant the dying man's soul rises to the gullet, human impotence breaks into full daylight. It is the moment of truth where all pretensions fall.

mots.racine: ب ل غ / ح ل ق
mots.sens: why not, when it reaches the throat

versets.trad: while you are present, looking on,
versets.commentaire: “And you, at that instant, look on.” The kin attend, powerless, the departure of the soul. They see but can do nothing: the spectacle of death unveils the absolute limits of human power.

mots.racine: ن ظ ر
mots.sens: you, at that instant, look on

versets.ar: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنكُمْ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تُبْصِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-naḥnu aqrabu ilayhi minkum wa-lākin lā tubṣirūna
versets.trad: and We are nearer to him than you, though you do not see,
versets.commentaire: “We are nearer to him than you, but you do not see.” Qushayrī makes of this verse a key of Sufism: the servant's greatest nearness to the Real comes when the remembrance and contemplation of God prevail in him; then his perception of all else fades, even his perception of himself, until he sees only the Real. Nearness and distance hold only in the state of wakefulness (ṣaḥw), as long as the servant is not ravished from himself; once ravished, there is neither near nor far.

mots.racine: ق ر ب
mots.sens: We are nearer to him than you

mots.racine: ب ص ر

versets.trad: why then, if you are not to be judged,
versets.commentaire: “Why do you not, if you are not subject to judgment...?” Qushayrī: you have no power over death. If man truly owed no account to anyone, let him prove his autonomy by holding back the soul that escapes. The challenge unmasks the illusion of independence.

mots.racine: د ي ن
mots.sens: if you are not subject to judgment

versets.trad: do you not bring that soul back, if you are truthful?
themes: the challenge to hold the soul
versets.commentaire: “...would you not bring back that soul, if you are truthful?” Qushayrī: to return the spirit to the body — an impossible proof. The challenge is unanswerable: none can hold back the departing breath. Impotence before death alone refutes every claim to dispense with God.

mots.racine: ر ج ع / ص د ق
mots.sens: you would bring it back, if truthful
mots.def: rajaʿa: to bring back; ṣādiq: truthful.

versets.trad: If he is of those brought near,
versets.commentaire: The sūra returns to the three groups to tell each one's lot at death. The brought-near (muqarrabūn). Qushayrī: these are those whom God has brought near by His grace. For them, death is no loss but an encounter: the term of the journey toward the long-desired nearness.

mots.racine: ق ر ب
mots.sens: if he is of the brought-near

versets.trad: then repose, fragrance, and a garden of delight;
versets.commentaire: For the brought-near: repose (rawḥ), fragrance (rayḥān), garden of delight. Qushayrī multiplies its spiritual senses: repose for their hearts, fragrance for their souls, garden for their bodies; repose: the breath of nearness; fragrance: the fullness of expansion; garden of delight: the place of intimate confiding. And the highest: repose: the vision of God; fragrance: the hearing of His word without intermediary; garden of delight: that it endure and never cease.

mots.racine: ر و ح
mots.def: rawḥ: repose, breeze; rayḥān: fragrance, sustenance.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ع م

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا إِن كَانَ مِنْ أَصْحَابِ الْيَمِينِ
versets.tr: wa-ammā in kāna min aṣḥābi l-yamīni
versets.trad: and if he is of the companions of the right,
themes: the lot of the people of the right
versets.commentaire: The second case: the people of the right. To each according to his rank: after the brought-near, those of the right too receive their share of peace. The hierarchy of the blessed excludes none from felicity.

mots.ar: وَأَمَّا إِن كَانَ مِنْ أَصْحَابِ الْيَمِينِ
mots.tr: wa-ammā in kāna min aṣḥābi l-yamīn
mots.racine: ي م ن
mots.sens: if he is of the people of the right

versets.trad: then: “Peace to you” from the companions of the right;
versets.commentaire: “Peace to you from the people of the right.” Qushayrī gives several readings: We announce to you the safety of their state; you will see in them the safekeeping you love; peace for you concerning them, burden not your heart with worry for them. Peace (salām) is the final note of their destiny: a heart set at rest forever.

mots.racine: س ل م / ي م ن
mots.sens: peace to you from the people of the right

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا إِن كَانَ مِنَ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ الضَّالِّينَ
versets.tr: wa-ammā in kāna mina l-mukadhdhibīna ḍ-ḍāllīna
versets.trad: but if he is of the straying deniers,
versets.commentaire: The third case: the straying deniers (mukadhdhibūn ḍāllūn). Qushayrī: if he is of those who denied God, straying from His religion. Denial and straying, already named at verse 51, seal their lot at death as they had sealed their life.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ض ل ل

versets.trad: then an entertainment of boiling water
versets.commentaire: For them, a welcome (nuzul) of boiling water. The same word nuzul as at verse 56: the hospitality offered to the denier is scalding water. The contrast with the repose and fragrance of the brought-near is total: to intimacy answers torment.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / ح م م

versets.trad: and a burning in the Blaze.
versets.commentaire: And a burning in the Blaze (jaḥīm). Qushayrī: if he is of the deniers, he has a sojourn in the Blaze. The term taṣliya denotes being delivered to the fire to burn in it: the torment is not external to the being, it consumes it.

mots.racine: ص ل ي / ج ح م
mots.def: taṣliya: the act of burning; jaḥīm: blazing fire.

versets.trad: This is the certain Truth.
versets.commentaire: “This is the Truth of certainty (ḥaqq al-yaqīn).” Qushayrī: it is the certain truth that will inevitably come about. The ḥaqq al-yaqīn is the highest degree of certainty — no longer knowledge (ʿilm) nor vision (ʿayn), but truth experienced. All the sūra has announced is of this order: not an opinion, but reality itself.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ي ق ن
mots.def: ḥaqq al-yaqīn: the truth of certainty.

versets.trad: So glorify the Name of your Lord, the Magnificent!
versets.commentaire: The sūra closes as at verse 74, on the glorification of the Name of the Lord, the Magnificent. Qushayrī: declare God free of what does not befit His description; some say: pray for God; some say: give thanks to God for preserving your community from straying and assisting it to follow your way. At the end of the great painting of the Last Things, only the tasbīḥ remains: to glorify the Name that abides when all passes away.

mots.racine: س ب ح / ع ظ م
mots.sens: glorify the Name of your Lord the Magnificent
